proofs.proof_tex:
Fix \(m\in\mathcal M_n\). For \(i\in U_n\), abbreviate \(A_i=Y_i(a)\), \(B_i=Y_i(b)\), \(p_i^a=\pi_i(a)\), and \(p_i^b=\pi_i(b)\). Exposure sufficiency and the fixed-schedule condition imply that the observed outcome equals \(A_i\) on \(\{I_i(a)=1\}\) and equals \(B_i\) on \(\{I_i(b)=1\}\). Hence
\[
X_i=\frac{I_i(a)A_i}{p_i^a}-\frac{I_i(b)B_i}{p_i^b}.
\]
Because \(a\ne b\), the two indicators are mutually exclusive. By the definition of the known exposure probabilities,
\[
\mathbb E_{P_Z}[X_i]
=\frac{p_i^a A_i}{p_i^a}-\frac{p_i^b B_i}{p_i^b}
=A_i-B_i.
\]
Target positivity justifies both divisions. Therefore \(W_i=X_i-(A_i-B_i)\) has mean zero. Summing the preceding identity and using the definitions of \(\widehat\theta\) and \(\theta\) gives
\[
\mathbb E_{P_Z}[\widehat\theta]
=\frac1n\sum_{i\in U_n}(A_i-B_i)=\theta,
\qquad
\sum_{i\in U_n}W_i
=\sum_{i\in U_n}X_i-\sum_{i\in U_n}(A_i-B_i)
=n(\widehat\theta-\theta).
\]

We next verify the dependency claim from the design primitives. For each \(i\), the variable \(X_i\) is measurable with respect to \(\sigma(Z_j:j\in N_i)\): the exposure indicator is determined by \(c_i(Z_{N_i})\), and exposure sufficiency makes the relevant observed outcome a fixed function of that exposure. Let \(H,K\subseteq U_n\) be disjoint vertex sets with no edge of \(D_n\) between them. By the definition of \(D_n\),
\[
\left(\bigcup_{i\in H}N_i\right)\cap
\left(\bigcup_{j\in K}N_j\right)=\varnothing.
\]
The Bernoulli design makes assignment coordinates indexed by these two disjoint unions independent. Thus \((X_i)_{i\in H}\) and \((X_j)_{j\in K}\) are independent. This is precisely the dependency-graph property for \((X_i)_{i\in U_n}\). Since each \(W_i\) differs from \(X_i\) by the fixed constant \(A_i-B_i\), the same property holds for \((W_i)_{i\in U_n}\).

It remains to establish the pointwise and second-moment bounds. On \(\{I_i(a)=1\}\),
\[
W_i=A_i\{(p_i^a)^{-1}-1\}+B_i\in[0,(p_i^a)^{-1}],
\]
whereas on \(\{I_i(b)=1\}\),
\[
W_i=-\left[A_i+B_i\{(p_i^b)^{-1}-1\}\right]
\in[-(p_i^b)^{-1},0].
\]
On every remaining exposure event, \(W_i=B_i-A_i\in[-1,1]\). Bounded outcomes, \(p_i^a,p_i^b\ge\pi_{\exp}\), and \(\pi_{\exp}\le1\) consequently give
\[
|W_i|\le\pi_{\exp}^{-1}.
\]
Finally, centering and mutual exclusivity yield
\[
\mathbb E_{P_Z}[W_i^2]
=\operatorname{Var}_{P_Z}(X_i)
\le \mathbb E_{P_Z}[X_i^2]
=\frac{A_i^2}{p_i^a}+\frac{B_i^2}{p_i^b}
\le\frac1{\pi_i(a)}+\frac1{\pi_i(b)}.
\]
Summing over \(i\) and applying \(\texttt{def:propensity-envelope}\) proves
\[
\sum_{i\in U_n}\mathbb E_{P_Z}[W_i^2]
\le\sum_{i\in U_n}\left\{\frac1{\pi_i(a)}+\frac1{\pi_i(b)}\right\}
=n\overline V_n.
\]

Fix \(m\in\mathcal M_n\), \(s\in\{-1,1\}\), and \(0<\lambda<3\pi_{\exp}/\kappa_n\). We first record the scalar moment calculation that will be used below. If \(W\) is centered, \(|W|\le M\), and \(0<t<3/M\), then the exponential power series, \(k!\ge 2\,3^{k-2}\) for every integer \(k\ge2\), and \(1+u\le e^u\) give
\[
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb E[e^{tsW}]
&=1+\sum_{k=2}^{\infty}\frac{t^k\mathbb E[(sW)^k]}{k!}\\\\
&\le 1+\sum_{k=2}^{\infty}\frac{t^k\mathbb E[|W|^k]}{k!}\\\\
&\le 1+\frac{t^2\mathbb E[W^2]}2
\sum_{k=2}^{\infty}\left(\frac{tM}{3}\right)^{k-2}\\\\
&\le \exp\left\{\frac{t^2\mathbb E[W^2]}{2(1-tM/3)}\right\}.
\end{aligned}
\]

By the exact fractional-color representation, choose an integer \(r_0\ge1\), let \(q=\chi_{r_0}(D_n)\), and choose independent vertex sets \(S_1,\ldots,S_q\) such that every vertex belongs to exactly \(r_0\) sets and
\[
\frac q{r_0}=\kappa_n.
\]
The centered-sum identity in \(\mathrm{lem{:}local\mbox{-}dependency}\) and the exact covering multiplicity imply
\[
\exp\left\{s\lambda n(\widehat\theta-\theta)\right\}
=\prod_{j=1}^q
\exp\left\{\frac{s\lambda}{r_0}\sum_{i\in S_j}W_i\right\}.
\]
We use the equal-exponent H\"older inequality in a self-contained form. For nonnegative \(A_1,\ldots,A_q\), the claim is trivial if some \(\mathbb E[A_j^q]=0\). Otherwise set \(B_j=A_j/\{\mathbb E[A_j^q]\}^{1/q}\). The arithmetic--geometric mean inequality gives \(\prod_{j=1}^q B_j\le q^{-1}\sum_{j=1}^q B_j^q\) pointwise, so expectation and rescaling give \(\mathbb E[\prod_j A_j]\le\prod_j\{\mathbb E[A_j^q]\}^{1/q}\). Applying this inequality to the preceding product gives
\[
\mathbb E_{P_Z}\!\left[e^{s\lambda n(\widehat\theta-\theta)}\right]
\le\prod_{j=1}^q
\left[
\mathbb E_{P_Z}\!\left\{
\exp\left(s\lambda\frac q{r_0}\sum_{i\in S_j}W_i\right)
\right\}
\right]^{1/q}.
\]
Each \(S_j\) is an independent set in the dependency graph, so the variables \((W_i)_{i\in S_j}\) are jointly independent. Apply the scalar bound with \(M=\pi_{\exp}^{-1}\) and \(t=\lambda q/r_0=\lambda\kappa_n\). The imposed range of \(\lambda\) ensures \(tM<3\). Thus
\[
\log\mathbb E_{P_Z}\!\left[
\exp\left(s\lambda\kappa_n\sum_{i\in S_j}W_i\right)
\right]
\le
\frac{\lambda^2\kappa_n^2}{2\{1-\lambda\kappa_n/(3\pi_{\exp})\}}
\sum_{i\in S_j}\mathbb E_{P_Z}[W_i^2].
\]
Taking logarithms in the H\"older bound, summing over color classes, and using that every vertex occurs exactly \(r_0\) times yield
\[
\begin{aligned}
\log\mathbb E_{P_Z}\!\left[e^{s\lambda n(\widehat\theta-\theta)}\right]
&\le
\frac{\lambda^2\kappa_n^2}{2q\{1-\lambda\kappa_n/(3\pi_{\exp})\}}
\sum_{j=1}^q\sum_{i\in S_j}\mathbb E_{P_Z}[W_i^2]\\\\
&=
\frac{\lambda^2\kappa_n^2r_0}{2q\{1-\lambda\kappa_n/(3\pi_{\exp})\}}
\sum_{i\in U_n}\mathbb E_{P_Z}[W_i^2]\\\\
&=
\frac{\lambda^2\kappa_n}{2\{1-\lambda\kappa_n/(3\pi_{\exp})\}}
\sum_{i\in U_n}\mathbb E_{P_Z}[W_i^2]\\\\
&\le
\frac{\lambda^2\kappa_n n\overline V_n}{2\{1-\lambda\kappa_n/(3\pi_{\exp})\}}.
\end{aligned}
\]
The last inequality is the total second-moment bound from \(\mathrm{lem{:}local\mbox{-}dependency}\). Subtracting the final deterministic upper bound inside the exponential is exactly the definition of \(\mathcal E_\lambda^s(\theta)\). Therefore
\[
\mathbb E_{P_Z}[\mathcal E_\lambda^s(\theta)]\le1.
\]

Fix \(m\in\mathcal M_n\) and set
\[
x=\log(2/\alpha),\qquad
v=\kappa_n n\overline V_n,
\qquad c=\frac{\kappa_n}{3\pi_{\exp}}.
\]
All three quantities are positive. The e-value inequality in \(\mathrm{lem{:}known\mbox{-}holder\mbox{-}mgf}\), together with its definition, is equivalent to
\[
\mathbb E_{P_Z}\!\left[
e^{s\lambda n(\widehat\theta-\theta)}
\right]
\le \exp\left\{\frac{\lambda^2v}{2(1-c\lambda)}\right\},
\qquad s\in\{-1,1\},\quad 0<\lambda<c^{-1}.
\]
Let \(u=\sqrt{2x/v}\) and choose
\[
\lambda_x=\frac{u}{1+cu}.
\]
Then \(0<\lambda_x<c^{-1}\) and \(1-c\lambda_x=(1+cu)^{-1}\). Markov's inequality applied to either sign, with
\[
t=\sqrt{2vx}+cx=vu+\frac{cvu^2}{2},
\]
therefore gives
\[
\begin{aligned}
\mathbb P_{Z,m}\!\left\{s n(\widehat\theta-\theta)\ge t\right\}
&\le
\exp\left\{-\lambda_xt+
\frac{\lambda_x^2v}{2(1-c\lambda_x)}\right\}\\\\
&=\exp\left\{-\frac{x(2+cu)}{1+cu}
+\frac{x}{1+cu}\right\}
=e^{-x}.
\end{aligned}
\]
Taking a union bound over \(s=1\) and \(s=-1\) yields
\[
\mathbb P_{Z,m}\!\left\{
|\widehat\theta-\theta|>
\sqrt{\frac{2\kappa_n\overline V_n x}{n}}
+\frac{\kappa_n x}{3n\pi_{\exp}}
\right\}
\le 2e^{-x}=\alpha.
\]
Substitution of \(x=\log(2/\alpha)\) gives the displayed probability bound in the proposition. By the definition of \(C_\alpha\), its complement event \(\{\theta\notin C_\alpha\}\) is exactly the strict-deviation event in the last display. Hence \(\mathbb P_{Z,m}\{\theta\in C_\alpha\}\ge1-\alpha\).

Suppose \(D_n\) has no edges. Then \(U_n\) itself is an independent set, so the one-fold coloring consisting only of \(S_1=U_n\) has \(q=r_0=1\). Hence \(\kappa_n\le1\). Conversely, in every exact \(r_0\)-fold coloring, any fixed vertex must belong to \(r_0\) sets among the \(q\) color classes, which forces \(q\ge r_0\). Thus every ratio \(q/r_0\) is at least one, and
\[
\kappa_n=\chi_f(D_n)=1.
\]

The public statement of \(\texttt{lem:local-dependency}\) says that \(D_n\) is a dependency graph for the centered exposure-HT summands. Since the whole vertex set is independent when \(D_n\) is edgeless, those summands are mutually independent. The same statement supplies \(\mathbb E_{P_Z}[W_i]=0\), \(|W_i|\le\pi_{\exp}^{-1}\), \(\sum_i\mathbb E_{P_Z}[W_i^2]\le n\overline V_n\), and \(\sum_iW_i=n(\widehat\theta-\theta)\). Substituting \(\kappa_n=1\) into \(\mathrm{prop{:}known\mbox{-}holder\mbox{-}bernstein\mbox{-}ci}\) gives
\[
\mathbb P_{Z,m}\!\left\{
|\widehat\theta-\theta|>
\sqrt{\frac{2\overline V_n\log(2/\alpha)}{n}}
+\frac{\log(2/\alpha)}{3n\pi_{\exp}}
\right\}\le\alpha.
\]
The generic independent-summand Bernstein radius for an average of centered summands with total variance envelope \(n\overline V_n\) and absolute range bound \(\pi_{\exp}^{-1}\) is exactly the radius in this display. Therefore the fractional-coloring interval reduces to the ordinary independent-summand Bernstein interval claimed.

prose_updates.tldr: For design-based exposure effects under Bernoulli network experiments, the paper pairs a computable fractional-chromatic Bernstein interval, valid over the full nonhomogeneous exposure class, with an exact homogeneous-witness transport that attains the effective rare-exposure count and yields a same-class expected-length sandwich; the concentration mechanism itself is established substrate, not the novelty claim.

project_justification.gap: The unresolved comparison is confidence-interval length for rare network exposures, not the existence of exposure-HT or dependency-graph concentration. Aronow--Samii provides the design-based exposure framework, exposure-HT estimation, and variance-based interference inference, but not this expected-length lower bound or same-class sandwich. Pelekis--Ramon--Wang provides the exact-fold Holder aggregation used on the upper side, and Janson (2004, Theorem 2.3) provides generic fractional-chromatic Bernstein-type large deviations for bounded partly dependent sums in terms of \(\chi^*\), summed variances, and a summand bound; neither supplies an exposure-HT causal interval, an e-value, the exact rare-revelation reduction, or the confidence-length comparison. Sandoval--Balakrishnan--Feller--Jordan--Waudby-Smith assumes SUTVA and ordinary direct two-arm outcomes under Bernoulli or complete randomization and proves information-theoretic minimax RMSE rates of order \(1/\sqrt{n\pi}\), rather than uniformly valid interval expected-length lower bounds carrying \(\sqrt{\log(1/\alpha)}\); it has neither network-exposure modeling nor the exact transport to independent rare reveals, although its effective-count intuition is consistent with \(B=n/r\), \(q=2^{-r}\), and rate \(1/\sqrt{Bq}\) after the present homogeneous-witness reduction. Leung (2022) studies approximate neighborhood interference and asymptotic inference; Harshaw--Middleton--Savje (2026) optimizes variance estimation under interference and complex designs; Ham et al. (2022) develops design-based confidence sequences; Kandiros et al. (2024) studies conflict-graph design and estimation under arbitrary neighborhood interference; Tchetgen Tchetgen--VanderWeele gives partial-interference benchmarks, including finite-sample concentration-based inference; and Rigdon--Hudgens gives exact binary-outcome intervals under two-stage partial interference. None of those results establishes the present fixed-confidence, balanced-Bernoulli clique-block, rare-exposure expected-length lower bound and attained same-class comparison. The scope distinction is essential: upper validity is over the larger nonhomogeneous clique-block class, whereas exact compression and the lower bound use only the homogeneous witness subclass.
project_justification.niche: The paper occupies the narrow junction between an established upper concentration mechanism and a new decision-theoretic witness argument. Aronow--Samii supplies the causal exposure and exposure-HT setting; exact-fold Holder aggregation from Pelekis--Ramon--Wang and generic \(\chi^*\)-Bernstein concentration from Janson (2004, Theorem 2.3) explain the upper tail substrate, for which no novelty is claimed. Sandoval--Balakrishnan--Feller--Jordan--Waudby-Smith supplies compatible \(1/\sqrt{n\pi}\) effective-count intuition under no interference, but its stated RMSE theorems do not imply a uniformly valid interval-length lower bound with \(\sqrt{\log(1/\alpha)}\), network exposures, or the exact rare-reveal transport; in the witness, \(B=n/r\) and \(q=2^{-r}\) recover the aligned scale \(1/\sqrt{Bq}\). The niche also differs from Leung's approximate-interference asymptotics, Harshaw--Middleton--Savje's variance-estimation optimization, Ham et al.'s confidence sequences, Kandiros et al.'s conflict-graph design problem, Tchetgen Tchetgen--VanderWeele's partial-interference concentration benchmarks, and Rigdon--Hudgens's exact two-stage binary-outcome intervals. Thus the niche is not a new fractional-color Bernstein theorem, a general-network minimax result, or separate necessity of graph and positivity penalties. Throughout this positioning, upper validity remains nonhomogeneous, while exact compression and lower-bound witnessing are homogeneous-subclass arguments.
project_justification.fill: The paper fills that niche by transporting a block-homogeneous witness exactly to independent rare revelations and using it to certify the lower endpoint of an attained expected-length sandwich, while the fractional-color interval supplies the upper endpoint over the larger nonhomogeneous clique-block class. The lower witness is a subclass argument: upper validity is genuinely nonhomogeneous, whereas exact compression and the lower bound use only homogeneous target schedules. Relative to Aronow--Samii, the added object is the lower bound and same-class comparison, not the exposure-HT framework. Relative to Pelekis--Ramon--Wang and Janson (2004, Theorem 2.3), the addition is the causal specialization together with exact witness transport and decision-theoretic interval-length analysis, not Holder or fractional-chromatic Bernstein concentration. Relative to Sandoval--Balakrishnan--Feller--Jordan--Waudby-Smith, the result is aligned with effective count \(Bq\) but is not a corollary because their SUTVA RMSE theorems do not furnish network-exposure interval-length risk or its \(\sqrt{\log(1/\alpha)}\) dependence. Relative to Leung, Harshaw--Middleton--Savje, Ham et al., Kandiros et al., Tchetgen Tchetgen--VanderWeele, and Rigdon--Hudgens, it adds the fixed balanced-Bernoulli rare-exposure lower-bound transport and same-class sandwich, rather than asymptotic approximate-interference inference, optimal variance estimation, confidence sequences, conflict-graph design, partial-interference concentration alone, or exact two-stage binary intervals.

prose_updates.related_work: Aronow--Samii is the foundation for design-based exposure mappings, exposure-HT estimators, and variance-based inference under interference; it does not prove the present confidence-length lower bound or attained same-class sandwich. The exact-fold Holder aggregation used for the upper bound comes from Pelekis--Ramon--Wang, and no novelty is claimed for that mechanism. Janson (2004), especially Theorem 2.3, gives generic Bernstein-type large-deviation bounds for bounded partly dependent sums using the fractional chromatic number \(\chi^*\), summed variances, and a uniform summand bound. Janson's theorem is therefore close upper-concentration substrate, but it is not an exposure-HT causal interval or e-value and contains neither the exact rare-revelation reduction nor a confidence-length lower or minimax comparison. Sandoval--Balakrishnan--Feller--Jordan--Waudby-Smith assumes SUTVA, with ordinary direct two-arm outcomes under Bernoulli or complete randomization, and proves information-theoretic minimax RMSE estimation rates of order \(1/\sqrt{n\pi}\). It does not prove uniformly valid confidence-interval expected-length lower bounds with the factor \(\sqrt{\log(1/\alpha)}\), and its stated model and theorems provide neither network-exposure modeling nor the exact transport to independent rare reveals. Nevertheless, after the homogeneous-witness reduction here, \(B=n/r\) and \(q=2^{-r}\) make its \(1/\sqrt{Bq}\) effective-count intuition fully consistent with the present scale; the present result is aligned with that intuition, not a corollary of its stated theorems. Leung (2022) treats approximate neighborhood interference and asymptotic inference, rather than a finite-sample rare-exposure expected-length sandwich. Harshaw--Middleton--Savje (2026) optimizes variance estimation under interference and complex designs, not confidence-length minimax risk or an exact rare-revelation reduction. Ham et al. (2022) develops design-based confidence sequences, not the fixed-confidence interference-class expected-length lower bound and same-class comparison here. Kandiros et al. (2024) studies conflict-graph experimental design and estimation under arbitrary neighborhood interference, rather than uniformly valid confidence-length risk for the fixed balanced-Bernoulli clique-block experiment. Tchetgen Tchetgen--VanderWeele supplies partial-interference causal benchmarks, including finite-sample concentration-based inference, but not this rare-exposure expected-length lower bound. Rigdon--Hudgens supplies exact binary-outcome intervals under two-stage partial interference, but not this lower bound or sandwich. Throughout, upper validity ranges over the larger nonhomogeneous clique-block class, while exact compression and the lower bound use only the homogeneous witness subclass. The contribution is the exact witness transport plus the attained same-class confidence-length sandwich, not fractional-color Bernstein concentration, a general-network minimax theorem, separate necessity of graph and exposure penalties, or a claim that prior effective-count intuition is inapplicable.
prose_updates.interpretation: The fractional-chromatic radius behaves as though the experiment had an effective number of independent exposure opportunities, but the paper's novelty claim is deliberately narrower than that heuristic. Aronow--Samii already supplies the exposure-HT causal framework and variance-based interference inference. Pelekis--Ramon--Wang supplies exact-fold Holder aggregation, and Janson (2004, Theorem 2.3) supplies generic \(\chi^*\)-adaptive Bernstein large deviations for bounded partly dependent sums; these are upper-bound substrates, not claimed discoveries. Sandoval--Balakrishnan--Feller--Jordan--Waudby-Smith proves \(1/\sqrt{n\pi}\) minimax RMSE behavior under SUTVA and ordinary two-arm Bernoulli or complete randomization. Its theorems do not directly yield network-exposure confidence-length risk with \(\sqrt{\log(1/\alpha)}\) or the exact independent-rare-reveal transport, even though substituting \(B=n/r\) and \(q=2^{-r}\) after the homogeneous-witness reduction gives the same effective-count intuition \(1/\sqrt{Bq}\). Leung's approximate-interference asymptotics, Harshaw--Middleton--Savje's optimal variance estimation, Ham et al.'s confidence sequences, Kandiros et al.'s conflict-graph design and estimation, Tchetgen Tchetgen--VanderWeele's partial-interference concentration benchmarks, and Rigdon--Hudgens's exact two-stage binary intervals address different inferential or design objects. The correct reading is therefore: the interval is valid on the full nonhomogeneous clique-block class, exact compression and the lower bound are established on its homogeneous witness subclass, and that witness certifies an attained same-class expected-length sandwich. It is not a new Holder--Bernstein mechanism, not a general-network minimax theorem, not proof that graph and exposure penalties are separately necessary, and not a rejection of earlier effective-count intuition.
prose_updates.technical_internal_limitation: The observable envelope replaces the schedule-specific design variance and can be conservative, while the generic upper radius combines fractional coloring and exposure positivity without proving that those two penalties are separately necessary on every network. The decision-theoretic lower analysis is certified through a balanced clique-block construction: the interval is valid over the larger nonhomogeneous block class, but exact data compression and the lower bound use only a block-homogeneous witness subclass. Consequently the sandwich is a same-class result by subclass witnessing, not a general-network minimax characterization. This boundary is compatible with the comparator map: Aronow--Samii covers exposure-HT foundations; Pelekis--Ramon--Wang and Janson cover the upper concentration substrate; Sandoval--Balakrishnan--Feller--Jordan--Waudby-Smith supplies aligned effective-count intuition under SUTVA but not the stated interval-length theorem; and Leung, Harshaw--Middleton--Savje, Ham et al., Kandiros et al., Tchetgen Tchetgen--VanderWeele, and Rigdon--Hudgens study, respectively, approximate-interference asymptotics, variance-estimation optimization, confidence sequences, conflict-graph design, partial-interference concentration, and exact two-stage binary intervals rather than this rare-reveal expected-length sandwich.
prose_updates.honest_scope: The finite-sample upper interval applies to bounded outcomes under known Bernoulli assignment, correct neighborhood exposure mappings, target-exposure positivity, and the stated exposure-dependency graph. The confidence-length comparison specializes to the canonical balanced clique-block class. Its upper validity covers nonhomogeneous schedules, whereas its exact reduction and lower bound deliberately use only homogeneous target schedules as witnesses. Aronow--Samii, Pelekis--Ramon--Wang, and Janson (2004, Theorem 2.3) already cover, respectively, the causal exposure-HT framework, exact-fold Holder aggregation, and generic fractional-chromatic Bernstein concentration. Sandoval--Balakrishnan--Feller--Jordan--Waudby-Smith gives aligned \(1/\sqrt{n\pi}\) effective-count RMSE intuition under SUTVA, but not this network-exposure interval-length result or its \(\sqrt{\log(1/\alpha)}\) factor. Leung (2022), Harshaw--Middleton--Savje (2026), Ham et al. (2022), Kandiros et al. (2024), Tchetgen Tchetgen--VanderWeele, and Rigdon--Hudgens do not state this fixed balanced-Bernoulli rare-revelation lower bound and same-class comparison. Accordingly, the claimed contribution is only the exact witness transport and the attained same-class expected-length sandwich; it excludes novelty for fractional-color Bernstein concentration, a general-network minimax theorem, separate necessity of graph and positivity penalties, and any assertion that prior effective-count intuition cannot apply conceptually.

statement_notes.justification: This lemma establishes exact design-unbiasedness and exposes the centered summands' dependency graph, range, and second-moment envelope. It is foundational exposure-HT algebra in the Aronow--Samii framework and carries no novelty claim.
statement_notes.gap: The lemma closes bookkeeping needed to specialize established fractional-color concentration to the causal exposure contrast; it is not a confidence-length lower bound, rare-reveal reduction, or same-class minimax comparison.
statement_notes.consumer: The Holder--Bernstein step consumes these explicit local bounds. In the paper-wide comparator map, Pelekis--Ramon--Wang and Janson supply concentration substrate, while the new contribution lies downstream in exact homogeneous-witness transport and the attained same-class interval-length sandwich. The interval remains valid over the larger nonhomogeneous clique-block class, while exact compression and lower-bound witnessing occur only on the homogeneous subclass.

statement_notes.justification: This lemma specializes exact-fold Holder aggregation to the exposure-HT summands. Pelekis--Ramon--Wang supplies that aggregation mechanism, and Janson (2004, Theorem 2.3) supplies closely related generic fractional-chromatic Bernstein concentration, so no novelty is claimed for the exponential mechanism itself.
statement_notes.gap: The lemma is not an exposure-class confidence-length lower bound. Aronow--Samii supplies the causal HT setting; Sandoval--Balakrishnan--Feller--Jordan--Waudby-Smith supplies aligned effective-count intuition under SUTVA; the paper's added step is the later exact rare-reveal witness and same-class sandwich.
statement_notes.consumer: It supplies the finite-sample upper benchmark. That role is distinct from Leung's asymptotic approximate-interference inference, Harshaw--Middleton--Savje's variance optimization, Ham et al.'s confidence sequences, Kandiros et al.'s conflict-graph design, Tchetgen Tchetgen--VanderWeele's partial-interference benchmarks, and Rigdon--Hudgens's exact two-stage binary intervals. The resulting upper validity extends over nonhomogeneous schedules; only the later exact reduction and lower-bound witness impose block homogeneity.

statement_notes.justification: Chernoff inversion gives a computable randomization-valid interval over the full nonhomogeneous exposure class. Its upper validity is useful, but the fractional-color Bernstein form is established substrate in light of Pelekis--Ramon--Wang and Janson (2004, Theorem 2.3), not the paper's priority claim.
statement_notes.gap: Aronow--Samii provides exposure-HT inference but not the later confidence-length lower bound. Sandoval--Balakrishnan--Feller--Jordan--Waudby-Smith proves SUTVA minimax RMSE rates, not uniformly valid expected interval length with \(\sqrt{\log(1/\alpha)}\), network exposures, or exact rare-reveal transport. Leung, Harshaw--Middleton--Savje, Ham et al., Kandiros et al., Tchetgen Tchetgen--VanderWeele, and Rigdon--Hudgens likewise address different asymptotic, variance, sequential, design, concentration, or exact-interval questions.
statement_notes.consumer: After restriction to the clique-block class and intersection with the causal parameter range, this proposition supplies the attainable upper endpoint. The matching lower endpoint uses only homogeneous witnesses inside the larger nonhomogeneous class, so the eventual novelty is the exact witness transport and same-class sandwich, not a general-network minimax claim or separate necessity of both penalties.

statement_notes.justification: This boundary check shows exact reduction to ordinary independent-summand Bernstein inference when \(\chi_f(D_n)=1\). It has no priority claim.
statement_notes.gap: The proposition verifies constants and dependence normalization only; it does not extend Aronow--Samii, Pelekis--Ramon--Wang, Janson, Sandoval--Balakrishnan--Feller--Jordan--Waudby-Smith, Leung, Harshaw--Middleton--Savje, Ham et al., Kandiros et al., Tchetgen Tchetgen--VanderWeele, or Rigdon--Hudgens on their respective causal, concentration, RMSE, asymptotic, variance, sequential, design, partial-interference, or exact-interval results.
statement_notes.consumer: It supports interpretation of the upper benchmark only. The paper's substantive addition remains the homogeneous-witness rare-reveal transport that certifies a lower bound against procedures valid on the larger nonhomogeneous class and thereby yields the attained same-class expected-length sandwich.

statement_notes.justification: The resolved comparison contributes the exact transport of a block-homogeneous witness to \(B=n/r\) independent rare-revelation coordinates with reveal probability \(q=2^{-r}\), and uses that transport to attain a same-class expected-length sandwich against intervals valid over the larger nonhomogeneous clique-block class. Aronow--Samii supplies the design-based exposure and exposure-HT framework and variance-based interference inference, not this confidence-length lower bound or sandwich. Pelekis--Ramon--Wang supplies the exact-fold Holder aggregation used as upper concentration substrate, with no novelty claimed for that mechanism. Janson (2004, Theorem 2.3) supplies generic fractional-chromatic Bernstein-type deviations for bounded partly dependent sums using \(\chi^*\), summed variances, and bounded summands, but not an exposure-HT interval or e-value, exact rare-revelation reduction, or interval-length lower comparison. Sandoval--Balakrishnan--Feller--Jordan--Waudby-Smith assumes SUTVA and ordinary direct two-arm outcomes under Bernoulli or complete randomization and proves minimax RMSE rates of order \(1/\sqrt{n\pi}\), rather than uniformly valid expected interval length with \(\sqrt{\log(1/\alpha)}\); its stated theorems contain neither network exposures nor the exact transport, although \(B=n/r\) and \(q=2^{-r}\) make its \(1/\sqrt{Bq}\) intuition consistent after the witness reduction. Leung treats approximate neighborhood interference and asymptotic inference; Harshaw--Middleton--Savje optimizes variance estimation under interference and complex designs; Ham et al. develops design-based confidence sequences; Kandiros et al. studies conflict-graph design and estimation under arbitrary neighborhood interference; Tchetgen Tchetgen--VanderWeele supplies partial-interference benchmarks including finite-sample concentration; and Rigdon--Hudgens supplies exact binary-outcome intervals under two-stage partial interference. None supplies this fixed balanced-Bernoulli rare-exposure expected-length sandwich.
statement_notes.gap: The theorem fills a decision-theoretic gap left by all ten comparators. It does not relabel Aronow--Samii's exposure-HT theory, Pelekis--Ramon--Wang's exact-fold Holder mechanism, or Janson's \(\chi^*\)-Bernstein theorem as new. It is aligned with, but is not a corollary of, Sandoval--Balakrishnan--Feller--Jordan--Waudby-Smith's SUTVA minimax RMSE results because the present object is uniformly valid confidence-interval expected length with \(\sqrt{\log(1/\alpha)}\), network exposure, and an exact rare-reveal experiment. It also differs from Leung's approximate-interference asymptotics, Harshaw--Middleton--Savje's variance-bound optimization, Ham et al.'s confidence sequences, Kandiros et al.'s conflict-graph design criterion, Tchetgen Tchetgen--VanderWeele's partial-interference concentration results, and Rigdon--Hudgens's exact two-stage binary intervals. Upper validity is over the nonhomogeneous clique-block class; exact compression and the lower bound use only its homogeneous witness subclass.
statement_notes.consumer: A downstream reader may use the theorem as an attained same-class confidence-length benchmark for the balanced clique-block experiment: the clipped fractional-color interval is valid over nonhomogeneous schedules, while the homogeneous witness subclass certifies the lower risk scale. The conclusion must not be exported as a new fractional-color Bernstein theorem, a minimax result for arbitrary networks, separate necessity of graph and exposure penalties, or evidence that prior effective-count intuition is conceptually inapplicable. Its novelty is exactly the witness transport plus the sandwich, with Aronow--Samii, Pelekis--Ramon--Wang, Janson, Sandoval--Balakrishnan--Feller--Jordan--Waudby-Smith, Leung, Harshaw--Middleton--Savje, Ham et al., Kandiros et al., Tchetgen Tchetgen--VanderWeele, and Rigdon--Hudgens retained in their distinct neighboring roles.
